one_line_takeaway: A robotics/controls paper (not an AI-security paper) proposing DualShield, which reuses one pre-computed HJ reachability value function both to steer a training-free diffusion motion planner and to certify its output via a CBVF-QP safety filter, achieving 100% success / 0% collision in a simulated interactive U-turn task versus 90%/10%, 0%/0%, and 80%/0% for MBD, NMPC, and DualGuard-MPPI baselines respectively.
key_finding: Table I (own results, not cited): DualShield achieves Ps(success rate)=100%, Pc(collision rate)=0%, lr,min=0.26m, Tm(completion time)=4.7s, jerk j=2.78 m/s^3, Tc(compute time/step)=0.78s. Baselines: Model-Based Diffusion (MBD) [Pan et al. 2024] gets Ps=90%, Pc=10%, lr,min=0.16m, Tm=4.3s, j=3.10, Tc=0.41s -- fails safety due to soft distance penalties under an adversarial HV that deviates from the constant-velocity prediction. Nonlinear MPC (NMPC, CasADi/IPOPT) gets Ps=0%, Pc=0%, Tm=not applicable (never completes), j=0.10, Tc=0.38s -- fails via local minima in the non-convex U-turn task. DualGuard-MPPI [Borquez et al. 2025] gets Ps=80%, Pc=0%, lr,min=0.24m, Tm=7.39s, j=6.40, Tc=0.24s -- safe but slower and jerkier due to local-perturbation sampling. DualShield is thus the only method with both 100% success and 0% collisions, and the shortest completion time among the safe methods, but it is also the slowest per-step (0.78s) of the four, attributed by the authors to the large number of value-function queries during sampling and shielding.
core_contribution: DualShield is a motion planning and control framework for autonomous driving that reuses a single pre-computed Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability value function in two roles: (1) as a proactive penalty term injected into the score function of a model-based (training-free) diffusion planner, steering the denoising process of a control-sequence distribution toward dynamically feasible, low-collision-risk trajectories, and (2) as a reactive safety filter, where the same value function is repurposed as a control barrier-value function (CBVF) and used to solve a real-time quadratic program (CBVF-QP) that minimally perturbs the diffusion planner's nominal first control action to guarantee forward invariance of the certified-safe set against worst-case (game-theoretic) maneuvers of surrounding human-driven vehicles (HVs). The value function is computed once, offline, in a relative (EV-body-frame) coordinate system so it can be reused across scenarios via real-time lookup rather than being recomputed online.
threat_model: Not a security threat model. The paper models an autonomous ego vehicle (EV) interacting with M human-driven vehicles (HVs) whose future actions are uncertain; safety is framed as a zero-sum differential/reachability game in which the EV must guarantee it can avoid a failure set F for all admissible HV control inputs uh in Uh, including a scripted 'adversarial' HV behavior mode (max-acceleration right-of-way contest) used purely as a worst-case driving-uncertainty scenario in simulation, not as an attack on a learned model, training pipeline, or software system. There is no adversary manipulating model inputs/weights/training data and no AI-security concept (e.g., prompt injection, poisoning, jailbreak, evasion) is present.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) DualShield's per-step computation time (0.78s) is slower than all three baselines, attributed to the volume of HJ value-function queries during diffusion sampling and QP shielding; they suggest GPU-parallel/JIT-compiled batched value queries as future mitigation. (2) The safety guarantee depends on a pre-computed, offline HJ value function restricted to pairwise (one EV-one HV) relative dynamics on a fixed low-dimensional (5D) grid; this pairwise-decomposition assumption and grid-based precomputation limit scalability to higher-dimensional dynamics or richer interaction structure. (3) The paper explicitly proposes future work to replace the pre-computed value function with an online, reinforcement-learning-based approximation to remove this precomputation dependency and improve generalization. (4) All results are from a single simplified 1:4-scale simulated U-turn scenario with simple point-mass/unicycle-like dynamics and only 2 HVs; no real-vehicle or more complex multi-agent (>2 HV) experiments are reported.

datasets_used: No dataset; a simulated 1:4-scaled interactive U-turn/merge driving scenario with 2 human-driven vehicles and 20 static obstacles, 100 trials over 10 randomly generated scenario configurations (10 trials each)
quantitative_results: DualShield (ours): success rate Ps=100%, collision rate Pc=0%, min relative distance lr,min=0.26m, completion time Tm=4.7s, jerk j=2.78 m/s^3, compute time Tc=0.78s per step
Model-Based Diffusion (MBD): Ps=90%, Pc=10%, lr,min=0.16m, Tm=4.3s, j=3.10, Tc=0.41s
Nonlinear MPC (NMPC): Ps=0%, Pc=0%, lr,min=0.28m, Tm=not reported (task not completed), j=0.10, Tc=0.38s
DualGuard-MPPI: Ps=80%, Pc=0%, lr,min=0.24m, Tm=7.39s, j=6.40, Tc=0.24s
HJ value function precomputation: ~3 hours on a 2.60GHz Intel Xeon Platinum 8358P CPU, over a 100x100x64x8x8 grid for a 5D relative state
baselines_compared: Model-Based Diffusion (MBD) [Pan, Yi, Shi, Qu, NeurIPS 2024] -- training-free diffusion planner with distance-based soft safety penalty
Nonlinear MPC (NMPC) -- CasADi/IPOPT gradient-based MPC with nonlinear obstacle-avoidance constraints
DualGuard-MPPI [Borquez, Raus, Ciftci, Bansal, IEEE RA-L 2025] -- sampling-based MPPI using the same HJ value function as a rejection sampler and switching-based safety filter
